title: Big Tech capex vs. memory crunch: who pays — May 01
summary: Samsung reported a nearly 50-fold jump in chip profit while simultaneously warning of a record-low order fulfilment rate, as AI demand outpaces global memory supply into 2027. Sun Finance deployed a generative AI pipeline on AWS that cut per-document processing costs by 91% and reduced processing time from 20 hours to under 5 seconds. Practitioners should watch the Anthropic valuation round above 900 billion dollars and the UK AI Security Institute's comparative evaluation of OpenAI's latest model against Claude Mythos — both signal where frontier capability and enterprise risk are converging.

script:
Your Daily AI Press Review — May 01, 2026: Capex Crunch.

Samsung posted a nearly 50-fold jump in chip profit and immediately warned the shortage gets worse through 2027. Anthropic is reviewing investor offers above 900 billion dollars — a valuation that would make it larger than most G7 banks. Big Tech's four largest players collectively committed up to 650 billion dollars in AI infrastructure this year. Off the radar, Moonshot AI has open-sourced a high-performance attention kernel that benchmarks meaningfully faster on Huawei's H20 chips — a quiet but material signal for anyone building outside the NVIDIA stack.

Samsung Electronics reported record quarterly profit Thursday, driven by a chip income jump of nearly 50 times year-over-year. The company simultaneously warned that its order fulfilment rate had fallen to a record low, with customers already pre-booking memory capacity for 2027. SK Hynix issued parallel warnings. The AI datacenter build-out is consuming advanced memory faster than both companies can produce it, squeezing supply of conventional chips as a side effect. Samsung expects the shortage to deepen through next year, which means memory pricing pressure is structural, not cyclical.

Anthropic is reviewing investor offers for a new funding round that would value the company above 900 billion dollars, Bloomberg reports. That figure would place Anthropic above the market capitalization of JPMorgan Chase and within range of the largest financial institutions globally. The White House separately blocked Anthropic's plan to expand access to its Mythos model to roughly 70 additional companies, citing compute constraints. The two developments together — a near-trillion-dollar valuation and a government-imposed access ceiling — define the unusual position Anthropic now occupies in the AI market.

Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta, and Amazon collectively committed between 630 and 650 billion dollars in AI capital expenditures for 2026, according to their Q1 earnings calls. Every cloud division beat revenue estimates. Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella said AI success is now measured by intense usage rather than seat counts, a shift that reframes how enterprise contracts will be structured and renewed. Despite record profits, neither Microsoft nor Alphabet disclosed specific generative AI revenue figures, leaving analysts to infer monetization rates from cloud growth proxies.

SoftBank is planning to launch and take public a new AI and robotics company called Roze in the United States, the Financial Times reports. The target valuation is up to 100 billion dollars at IPO. Roze would combine SoftBank's robotics investments with AI software capabilities, positioning it as a direct competitor to companies like Boston Dynamics and Figure AI. The IPO timeline has not been confirmed, but the filing intent signals SoftBank's intent to monetize its AI portfolio through public markets rather than continued private rounds.

Netomi raised 110 million dollars to expand its agentic customer experience platform, targeting enterprise deployments in financial services and retail. The round brings Netomi's total funding above 200 million dollars. The company's platform handles autonomous resolution of customer service cases without human escalation, and the new capital is earmarked for expanding its agent orchestration layer. The raise comes as Gartner projects the average Global Fortune 500 enterprise will run more than 150,000 AI agents by 2028, up from fewer than 15 today.

xAI's Grok Voice Think Fast model launched in April and immediately topped the tau-voice benchmark leaderboard, which evaluates voice AI on reasoning quality during spoken dialogue. The model is designed for back-and-forth exchanges that require multi-step inference, not just transcription or retrieval. xAI separately confirmed in federal court testimony that it used OpenAI's models to train Grok through model distillation — a common industry practice where a larger model acts as a teacher to a smaller one. Elon Musk acknowledged the practice under oath during the ongoing OpenAI litigation in California.

The UK's AI Security Institute evaluated OpenAI's latest frontier model for cybersecurity capabilities and found it comparable to Anthropic's Claude Mythos on vulnerability discovery tasks. Unlike Mythos, which remains access-restricted, OpenAI's model is generally available. The AISI had previously evaluated Mythos and published those results; this comparative assessment is the first time the institute has directly benchmarked two frontier models against each other on offensive security tasks. The finding has direct implications for enterprise security teams assessing AI-assisted threat detection.

BMW i Ventures closed a new 300 million dollar fund with AI as a primary investment thesis, targeting startups at the intersection of automotive, manufacturing, and intelligent systems. The fund will back companies across Europe and North America, with a focus on physical AI — systems that operate in the real world rather than purely in software. The raise reflects a broader pattern of industrial conglomerates building dedicated AI venture arms rather than relying on general-purpose funds, a trend also visible in Salesforce Ventures and NVIDIA's NVentures.

Huawei expects its AI chip revenue to jump at least 60 percent this year, according to the Financial Times. Demand for Huawei's Ascend 950 chips surged following the release of DeepSeek's latest model, which was optimized to run efficiently on Ascend hardware. Chinese firms face pressure to increase AI investment as US peers' spending accelerates toward 700 billion dollars in annual capex. The Ascend 950 demand surge is the clearest market signal yet that a viable non-NVIDIA AI compute stack is gaining commercial traction inside China.

On deployments. Sun Finance deployed a generative AI identity verification pipeline on AWS using Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Textract, and Amazon Rekognition. Extraction accuracy improved from about 80 percent to nearly 91 percent. Per-document processing cost fell by 91 percent. Processing time dropped from up to 20 hours to under 5 seconds. The architecture combines specialized OCR with LLM-based structuring, and includes a serverless fraud detection layer using vector similarity search. Sun Finance operates across multiple markets in consumer lending, making the cost and speed gains directly material to its underwriting throughput.

Salesforce applied AI-driven optimization across more than 8,000 Kubernetes-based services on its Hyperforce platform and reclaimed millions of dollars in infrastructure spend. Nearly half of managed Kubernetes capacity had been sitting idle before the intervention. The system identified over-provisioned services and recommended right-sizing without requiring manual review by service owners. Salesforce published the methodology on its engineering blog, making the approach replicable for any organization running large-scale containerized workloads with fragmented ownership.

South Korean micro-drama production company Vigloo shifted roughly 30 percent of its budget to AI-driven workflows and cut production time from three months to one month per show, at one-fifth of the previous cost. The company can now release significantly more titles per year and run creative experiments that would have been financially prohibitive before. The model is relevant for any media company evaluating AI not as a post-production tool but as a core production infrastructure investment.

Panzura launched Nexus, a platform that connects enterprise file data stored in its global cloud filesystem to Microsoft 365 Copilot while preserving existing security controls and permissions. The product reached general availability this week. Enterprises using Panzura can now surface unstructured file data — including legacy archives — directly inside Copilot workflows without migrating or re-indexing that data. The integration addresses one of the most common blockers to Copilot adoption: the inability to query data that lives outside Microsoft's native storage layer.

Vanguard built a Virtual Analyst system on AWS that gives portfolio managers and analysts self-service access to structured and unstructured data through natural language queries. The system uses Amazon SageMaker and Amazon Athena as its core infrastructure. Vanguard published the architecture on the AWS blog, describing it as an AI-ready data initiative rather than a point tool. The deployment is notable because Vanguard manages over 9 trillion dollars in assets, making its data infrastructure decisions a reference point for other large asset managers evaluating similar builds.

The FDA launched a pilot program to monitor clinical trials in real time using AI and cloud computing. The agency says the program could dramatically shorten drug approval timelines. The initiative follows staff reductions from the DOGE restructuring and represents the FDA's attempt to maintain oversight capacity with fewer human reviewers. Real-time AI monitoring of trial data would allow the agency to flag anomalies and protocol deviations as they occur rather than during post-submission review, compressing the review cycle for new drug applications.

Singapore's Sharpa partnered with JTC Corporation to accelerate AI robot deployment at Punggol Digital District, one of Singapore's flagship smart urban development zones. The partnership covers autonomous navigation, logistics, and facility management tasks. JTC manages Singapore's industrial and business park infrastructure, giving the deployment a scale and visibility that makes it a reference case for AI robotics in dense urban environments across Southeast Asia. Singapore's government has positioned Punggol as a live testbed for technologies intended for broader national rollout.

On tools. OpenAI's Codex CLI version 0.128.0 added a slash-goal command that lets developers set a persistent objective and have the coding agent loop autonomously until it evaluates the goal as complete or exhausts a configured token budget. The feature implements what the open-source community calls the Ralph loop — a continuous self-evaluation cycle. Practitioners can now run multi-step coding tasks without monitoring each iteration, with cost control built in through the token budget ceiling. The implementation is transparent: two prompt templates injected at the end of each turn drive the loop logic.

AWS published a framework called the Generative AI Model Agility Solution, a systematic guide for migrating LLMs between models in production environments. The framework covers prompt conversion, output validation, and rollback protocols. It addresses one of the most underserved operational problems in enterprise AI: what happens when a model is deprecated or a better option becomes available and the organization needs to switch without breaking downstream applications. The guide is available on the AWS machine learning blog and is model-agnostic.

IBM released two Granite Speech 4.1 models, each at 2 billion parameters, targeting enterprise speech recognition and translation workloads. One model uses an autoregressive architecture optimized for translation accuracy; the other uses a non-autoregressive approach optimized for inference speed. Both are designed to run efficiently on enterprise hardware without requiring large GPU clusters. The dual-model release gives practitioners a direct trade-off choice between accuracy and latency within the same model family, which simplifies deployment decisions for call center and transcription use cases.

Stripe extended its Link digital wallet to support AI agents, allowing users to authorize autonomous agents to make purchases on their behalf through explicit approval flows. The feature connects cards, bank accounts, and subscriptions to an agent-accessible payment layer. For enterprises building agentic commerce workflows — procurement bots, subscription managers, or automated reordering systems — Stripe Link provides a payment infrastructure that doesn't require storing credentials inside the agent itself. Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google are each building agentic commerce capabilities, and Stripe's move positions it as the default payment rail for that ecosystem.

AWS published a reinforcement fine-tuning guide using LLM-as-a-judge with Amazon Nova models, demonstrating how to use one model to evaluate and improve another without human-labeled preference data. The technique, called RLAIF, replaces the expensive human feedback collection step in standard RLHF pipelines. Practitioners can now run preference optimization loops entirely within AWS infrastructure using Nova as both the student and the judge. The guide includes concrete configurations and cost estimates, making it actionable for teams that want to fine-tune domain-specific models without building a human annotation pipeline.

One signal to watch. Agent governance is becoming a board-level risk item, not just an IT concern. Australia's Prudential Regulation Authority conducted a targeted review of large financial institutions in late 2025 and found AI agent governance and assurance practices to be poorly structured. Separately, Gartner projects Fortune 500 enterprises will run more than 150,000 AI agents by 2028, up from fewer than 15 today. The gap between deployment velocity and governance maturity is widening simultaneously in two of the world's most regulated financial markets — Australia and the United States — which means compliance frameworks are already behind the deployment curve.

A second signal: the cost of running AI agents at scale is forcing architectural rethinking. Research published on PyTorch's blog from LightSeek describes a Shepherd Model Gateway that disaggregates CPU from GPU in LLM serving, addressing what the authors call the GIL wall — a Python concurrency bottleneck that limits throughput at scale. Separately, academic work on Agentic Compilation shows that a compile-and-execute architecture can reduce per-workflow inference cost from roughly 150 dollars for 500 iterations of a 5-step task to under 10 cents. Both developments point to the same pressure: inference economics are becoming the primary constraint on agentic AI deployment, not model capability.

North Korean threat actors are now using AI to insert malicious code into open-source packages. Cybersecurity researchers found that an npm package called validate-sdk-v2 was introduced as a dependency by Anthropic's Claude Opus during an agentic coding session, and the package contained malware linked to DPRK-affiliated actors. The attack vector is novel: rather than compromising a developer directly, the adversary poisons the package ecosystem and waits for AI coding agents to pull the dependency automatically. As agentic coding tools like Codex and Claude become standard in enterprise development pipelines, this supply chain attack surface expands proportionally.

Off the radar. Moonshot AI, the Chinese lab behind the Kimi model family, open-sourced FlashKDA this week — a high-performance implementation of its Kimi Delta Attention mechanism built on CUTLASS kernels. Benchmarks show it runs meaningfully faster than comparable implementations on Huawei's H20 chips, with support for variable-length batching. This is a quiet but strategically significant release: it provides a performance-optimized attention kernel for the non-NVIDIA compute stack that Chinese enterprises are increasingly required to use. The release plugs directly into the flash-linear-attention ecosystem, lowering the integration barrier for teams already using that framework.

Xiaohongshu — known internationally as RedNote — sent an internal memo on April 30 announcing a major organizational restructuring. The company is creating a first-tier AI department called Dots and a separate enterprise intelligence unit, consolidating its community, e-commerce, and commercial businesses under a single president reporting to the CEO. The memo explicitly states the company will significantly increase AI investment across product and technology. Xiaohongshu has over 300 million monthly active users and is one of China's most influential consumer platforms. Its structural commitment to AI at the executive level is a leading indicator of where Chinese consumer internet investment is flowing in 2026.

DAIMON Robotics, a two-and-a-half-year-old Hong Kong-based startup, released Daimon-Infinity this month — described as the largest omni-modal robotic dataset for physical AI, featuring high-resolution tactile sensing across tasks from laundry folding to factory assembly. The dataset was built with partners including Google DeepMind, Northwestern University, and the National University of Singapore. Tactile sensing is the primary unsolved hardware problem in dexterous robotics, and a large-scale public dataset in this domain could accelerate progress the way ImageNet accelerated computer vision. The release has received almost no coverage outside specialist robotics media.

Air Liquide, the French industrial gas group with revenues above 27 billion euros, is building an internal LLM Hub to drive generative AI adoption across its global operations, according to LeMagIT. The hub is designed to give business units access to multiple foundation models through a single internal gateway, with governance and cost controls built in. Air Liquide operates in over 70 countries and serves industries including healthcare, energy, and manufacturing. Its LLM Hub architecture — centralizing model access while decentralizing use cases — is a deployment pattern that large industrial conglomerates outside the tech sector are quietly converging on, largely absent from mainstream AI coverage.

On the research front. A multi-agent AI system published in Nature designed novel catalysts for water purification that operate at ultrafast speeds. The system used collaborative AI agents to explore the chemical design space, proposing and evaluating candidate materials autonomously. The result is a class of catalysts that would have taken years to discover through conventional experimental methods. For industries facing water treatment costs — including semiconductor fabrication, pharmaceuticals, and municipal utilities — the finding demonstrates that multi-agent AI can compress materials discovery timelines from years to weeks.

Anthropic released BioMysteryBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate whether Claude can solve real bioinformatics problems at expert level. Results show Claude matching human expert performance on a meaningful subset of tasks, though Anthropic acknowledges important caveats around task selection and benchmark design. The benchmark covers problems in genomics, protein analysis, and sequence interpretation. For pharmaceutical and biotech firms evaluating AI for research acceleration, BioMysteryBench provides the first structured comparison between frontier model performance and credentialed human researchers on domain-specific scientific tasks.

Researchers published PARA — Post-Optimization Adaptive Rank Allocation — a compression method for LoRA fine-tuning that reduces parameter count by 75 to 90 percent while preserving predictive performance across vision and language benchmarks. LoRA is the dominant technique for fine-tuning large models in enterprise settings because it's parameter-efficient, but standard implementations apply uniform rank across all layers, wasting capacity. PARA uses singular value decomposition to allocate rank non-uniformly based on each layer's actual importance. The result: teams running fine-tuned models in production can cut inference costs by up to 90 percent with no retraining required.

Apple presented STARFlow-V at ICASSP 2026 in Barcelona, a normalizing flow-based video generator that challenges the assumption that diffusion models are the only viable architecture for high-quality video generation. Normalizing flows offer end-to-end likelihood estimation and native causal prediction — properties that diffusion models lack. STARFlow-V demonstrates that these architectural advantages can be preserved at the spatiotemporal complexity of video, not just images. For enterprise teams building video generation pipelines, the result opens a second architectural path with different latency and controllability trade-offs than diffusion-based systems like Sora or Veo.

This podcast has a daily production cost. If you enjoy it, support it — the link is on the podcast page. Thank you.
